subUnits.title: 4.1 Basic Concepts and Collision Theory
notes: Definition: A process in which one or more substances (reactants) are converted to new substances (products) with different properties.
• Reactants: Starting materials that undergo change
• Chemical bonds in reactants are broken
• New chemical bonds are formed in products
Definition: The speed at which reactants are converted to products.
Units: mol/dm³/s or mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹
• Occur over minutes, hours, days, or years
The collision theory explains how chemical reactions occur and why reaction rates differ.
- For a reaction to occur, reactant particles must collide with each other
- Colliding particles must have minimum energy called ACTIVATION ENERGY (Ea)
- Activation Energy: Minimum energy required for a reaction to occur
- Collisions with energy < Ea are ineffective (no reaction)
- Collisions with energy ≥ Ea are effective (reaction occurs)
- Particles must collide in the correct spatial orientation
- Proper orientation allows bonds to break and form correctly
- Incorrect orientation = no reaction even with sufficient energy
• Collisions that result in a chemical reaction
Factors Affecting Number of Effective Collisions:
• Energy barrier that must be overcome for reaction to occur
• Catalysts lower Ea, increasing reaction rate
Shows energy changes during a reaction
diagramDescriptions: Collision theory diagram showing effective vs ineffective collisions with proper and improper orientations
Energy profile diagram showing activation energy (Ea), reactants, products, and transition state
Diagram comparing fast and slow reactions with different activation energies

Comparison of catalyzed vs uncatalyzed reaction showing lowered activation energy
subUnits.youtubeQuery: Collision theory activation energy chemical reactions SSS2 chemistry site:youtube.com

quiz.q: According to collision theory, for a reaction to occur, particles must:
opts: Collide with sufficient energy and proper orientation
quiz.explanation: Collision theory states that particles must collide with both sufficient energy (≥ Ea) and proper orientation for a reaction to occur.

quiz.q: Activation energy is:
quiz.explanation: Activation energy (Ea) is the minimum energy that colliding particles must have for a reaction to occur.

quiz.q: A reaction with high activation energy will be:
quiz.explanation: High activation energy means fewer particles have sufficient energy, resulting in a slower reaction.

quiz.q: Effective collisions are those that:
opts: Have sufficient energy and proper orientation
quiz.explanation: Effective collisions result in chemical reactions and require both sufficient energy and proper orientation.

quiz.q: Which of the following is a fast reaction?
quiz.explanation: Neutralization reactions are fast, occurring in seconds. Rusting, photosynthesis, and fermentation are slow reactions.

quiz.q: The rate of a chemical reaction is defined as:
opts: Change in concentration per unit time
quiz.explanation: Reaction rate = Change in concentration / Time (units: mol/dm³/s)

quiz.q: Particles with energy less than activation energy will:
quiz.explanation: Particles with energy < Ea cannot overcome the energy barrier and will not react.

quiz.q: The transition state in a reaction is also called:
quiz.explanation: The transition state or activated complex is the high-energy, unstable arrangement of atoms at the peak of the energy barrier.

quiz.q: Which factor does NOT affect the number of effective collisions?
quiz.explanation: Color does not affect collision frequency or effectiveness. Concentration, temperature, and surface area do affect effective collisions.

quiz.q: In an energy profile diagram, the difference between reactant and product energy is:
quiz.explanation: ΔH (enthalpy change) = Energy of products - Energy of reactants

subUnits.title: 4.2 Factors Affecting Reaction Rate and Types of Reactions
notes: - Concentrated HCl reacts faster with Mg than dilute HCl
- For every 10°C rise in temperature, reaction rate approximately doubles
- Food spoils faster at room temperature than in refrigerator
- Reactions are heated to speed them up
• Increasing surface area increases reaction rate
- Powdered CaCO₃ reacts faster with HCl than lump CaCO₃
• A substance that increases reaction rate without being consumed
• Provides alternative pathway with lower activation energy
• Not used up in the reaction
- Haber process uses high pressure (200 atm)
• Ionic compounds react faster than covalent compounds
• Reason: Ionic reactions involve ion attraction (fast)
• Energy of products > Energy of reactants
- Thermal decomposition: CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂  ΔH = +ve
• Energy of products < Energy of reactants
- Neutralization: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O  ΔH = -ve
• Only changes in enthalpy (ΔH) can be measured
diagramDescriptions: Graph showing reaction rate vs concentration (directly proportional)
Graph showing reaction rate vs temperature (exponential increase)
Diagram comparing powdered vs lump solid showing increased surface area
Energy profile diagram comparing catalyzed vs uncatalyzed reaction
Energy profile diagram for endothermic reaction showing ΔH positive
Energy profile diagram for exothermic reaction showing ΔH negative
Comparison chart of endothermic vs exothermic reactions with examples
subUnits.youtubeQuery: Factors affecting reaction rate endothermic exothermic SSS2 chemistry site:youtube.com

quiz.q: Increasing the concentration of reactants increases reaction rate because:
quiz.explanation: Higher concentration means more particles per unit volume, leading to more frequent collisions and faster reaction.

quiz.q: For every 10°C rise in temperature, reaction rate approximately:
quiz.explanation: As a rule of thumb, reaction rate approximately doubles for every 10°C increase in temperature.

quiz.q: Powdered CaCO₃ reacts faster with HCl than lump CaCO₃ because:
quiz.explanation: Powdered solid has larger surface area, exposing more particles for collision, increasing reaction rate.

quiz.q: A catalyst increases reaction rate by:
quiz.explanation: Catalysts provide an alternative pathway with lower activation energy, increasing the number of effective collisions.

quiz.q: In an endothermic reaction, ΔH is:
quiz.explanation: Endothermic reactions absorb heat, so ΔH = H(products) - H(reactants) is positive.

quiz.q: Combustion reactions are:
quiz.explanation: Combustion reactions release heat to surroundings, making them exothermic (ΔH is negative).

quiz.q: Photosynthesis is an example of:
quiz.explanation: Photosynthesis absorbs energy from sunlight, making it endothermic (ΔH = +2800 kJ/mol).

quiz.q: Which factor does NOT affect the rate of reaction between a solid and a solution?
quiz.explanation: Pressure affects reactions involving gases. For solid-solution reactions, concentration, temperature, and surface area are relevant.

quiz.q: In an exothermic reaction:
opts: Products have more energy than reactants
Reactants have more energy than products
quiz.explanation: In exothermic reactions, reactants have more energy than products, and the difference is released as heat (ΔH is negative).

quiz.q: Enzymes in biological reactions act as:
quiz.explanation: Enzymes are biological catalysts that speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.
